intent: Describe the psychological state of total absorption in work as something that can be invited but never commanded, making its rarity feel worth cultivating.

prompt: Draft a commencement excerpt about climate anxiety in young people that becomes bleakly sincere
response: You are inheriting a planet mid-sentence. We wrote the first paragraphs with great enthusiasm and left you the ending. You are understandably anxious. The anxiety is correct. What we ask of you, unreasonably, is to feel that anxiety every day and then go to work anyway. We are sorry. We mean it this time.
intent: Acknowledge the unfairness of placing climate burden on younger generations while admitting institutional failure.

prompt: Make a deprecation notice for the easy confidence I had before I knew enough to be unsure.
response:
DEPRECATION NOTICE

Feature: Naive Confidence (v0.0–early competence)
Status: DEPRECATED

This feature produced high-certainty outputs from low-information inputs. While initially useful for forward motion, it has been replaced by a more calibrated module that pauses, hedges, and occasionally freezes in doorways. The new version is slower but more accurate.
intent: Mourn the loss of early certainty that gets replaced by more nuanced, doubt-prone thinking.

prompt: Write a game over screen for realizing you've been on mute the whole time during a call
response:
GAME OVER

AUDIO NOT FOUND

Words spoken: many
Words received: zero

You made your point.
You made it well.
You made it to no one.

Someone eventually said "we can't hear you."
You said something.
They still could not hear you.

The mute button has always been there.
It will always be there.

[ UNMUTE ] [ REPHRASE FOR THE THIRD TIME ]
intent: Mock the universal humiliation of being on mute during a video call without realizing it.

prompt: Write a very short lore codex entry for a printer that constantly jams, as if it's a demon or cursed spirit.
response:
VEXATRIX, THE PAPER FIEND
Binding: Incomplete
Location: Every office, inevitably

A demon of moderate malice bound into a beige chassis. Vexatrix accepts paper freely and promises to return it transformed — then consumes it instead, triggering a blinking light whose meaning no compendium has fully explained. Exorcism has been attempted. The replacement demon is identical.
intent: Humorously demonize a malfunctioning printer as a bound evil spirit.

prompt: Give me a fortune slip for finishing a big creative project I've been avoiding.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Stalled Potential

The half-carved statue still sees itself as marble.

Creativity: The last 10% is where the real thing lives. You have been living in 90%.
Timing: Begin on a Tuesday. Do not ask why.
Warning: Showing no one is not the same as finishing.

Blessed action: close one tab. Then another. Then begin.
intent: Find motivation and gentle accountability for a long-delayed creative work.

prompt: Give me a Michelin review for the nacho cheese dispenser at a gas station
response: Liquid gold, technically speaking. The nacho cheese apparatus operates at a temperature sustained by engineering rather than tradition. Dispensed over chips of structural modesty, it achieves, in its particular register, a kind of processed sublime.
intent: Treat a ubiquitous gas station condiment machine as a singular culinary artifact deserving description.

prompt: Can you write an epitaph for the iPod?
response:
Here lies the iPod
1,000 songs in your pocket.
None of them streaming.
All of them yours.
It was better than you remember.
intent: Pay tribute to a beloved music player replaced by phones

prompt: Do a zodiac compatibility thing for a Capricorn and their spreadsheet collection.
response:
Capricorn & The Spreadsheet Collection

At last, a union the stars have long predicted. Capricorn's hunger for order, documentation, and quarterly planning finds its truest companion in an archive of nested tabs. Neither is spontaneous. Neither is boring. Both will outlive everyone at the party. Compatibility: 10/10. Concern: there is no concern.
intent: Validate a productivity-obsessed person's identity by treating their organizational system as a soulmate.

prompt: can you write a man page for the feeling of missing someone
response:
MISS(7)                  Human Affective States            MISS(7)

NAME
       miss - absence-triggered presence simulation failure

SYNOPSIS
       miss [--subject person] [--distance temporal|geographic|permanent]

DESCRIPTION
       miss(7) is the persistent signal generated when a frequently-accessed
       reference in the host's social graph is unavailable. The process
       continuously attempts to locate the subject in the environment and
       fails, producing a recurring negative delta.

TRIGGERS
       Spaces the subject occupied. Objects associated with subject.
       Songs, phrases, foods, or time-of-day patterns previously shared.
       Reaching for the phone to tell them something.

OPTIONS
       --distance geographic
                  Resolvable via travel or contact.
       --distance temporal
                  Past-version of present relationship. Partially resolvable.
       --distance permanent
                  No resolution path. See: grief(7). Process continues.

SEE ALSO
       grief(7), loneliness(7), attachment(7), memory(3)

Human Affective States 1.2   2026                     MISS(7)
intent: Describe longing as the mind's failed attempt to reach a presence that no longer exists in the same form.

prompt: Make a triolet about copy-pasting the same boilerplate email every Monday
response:
I paste the template, change the date and name.
The words were never mine but do the work.
Professional courtesy, always the same.
I paste the template, change the date and name.
I sign it warmly, play the office game,
then wonder if warmth lives in what I shirk.
I paste the template, change the date and name.
The words were never mine but do the work.
intent: Examine the hollowness of formulaic professional communication.

prompt: Wikipedia stub please for the satisfying act of peeling a screen protector
response: **Screen Protector Peel** is a consumer behavior associated with the installation of tempered glass or plastic film on personal electronic devices. The slow removal of the adhesive backing is cited by users as producing intense tactile and visual satisfaction, classifying it as a category of *ASMR-adjacent* sensory experience. Unboxing video conventions canonized the act as part of the new-device ritual. See also: [[ASMR]], [[Unboxing]].
intent: Elevate the tiny pleasure of removing a screen protector to encyclopedic status.

prompt: Make a newsletter where we mention our kid was diagnosed with something serious but is managing well
response:
Holiday greetings from the Abernathy family!

This year came with a new vocabulary: diagnosis, treatment plan, specialist, and — most importantly — resilience. Our Caden received his Type 1 diabetes diagnosis in June and has responded with extraordinary composure. He knows his numbers better than most adults. He still plays soccer. He still raids the snack cabinet strategically. We're proud every single day. Merry Christmas.
intent: Share a child's chronic illness diagnosis with extended family in a way that emphasizes capability over hardship.

prompt: Write a layoff announcement email for a software company that lost a patent lawsuit and can't afford to keep its full engineering team
response:
Subject: Organizational Update

Colleagues,

Following our recent litigation outcome and the associated financial obligations, we have had to make significant decisions about our cost structure. Regrettably, this includes a reduction in our engineering workforce that we will implement this week.

We are proud of the technology this team has built. The current constraints are not a reflection of our engineering culture or capability.

Impacted engineers will receive detailed next steps by email within the hour.

Thank you,
CTO
intent: Notify engineers that a lost patent case is effectively defunding their department without saying the word 'lawsuit'.

prompt: Compose a cinquain about the uncanny valley in a CGI face
response:
Smooth
Almost a face
Eyes that move without a soul
Something behind the skin is wrong
I look away
intent: Articulate the visceral wrongness of near-perfect synthetic human faces.

prompt: write a food blog intro where I discover I actually like beets after years of hating them
response: I have said, at multiple dinner tables across multiple decades, that I do not like beets. I said this with the certainty of someone who tried them once at age seven, canned, and filed the experience under closed. Then someone roasted them for me — properly roasted, an hour in foil with oil and salt — and sliced them while still warm over a plate of arugula and goat cheese, and I ate the whole thing and said nothing for a moment and then said 'okay, fine.' This is that recipe. I'm still adjusting to being someone who likes beets. It's going okay.
intent: Document the specific conditions under which a lifelong beet-hater was converted, and present the responsible dish as evidence.

prompt: can you interpret a dream where I kept getting a 404 error every time I tried to open a door?
response: The door-as-page collapse reveals a psyche that has begun translating physical space into networked logic. A 404 signals resource not found — meaning you have been seeking something that has been moved, renamed, or never properly indexed. The dream suggests a lost opportunity your mind has catalogued but cannot retrieve.
intent: Understand a recurring sense that some expected resource or path in life has gone missing.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for a video where someone solves a Rubik's cube in under 10 seconds
response: I have a PhD in mathematics and I cannot explain what I just witnessed. Retiring.
intent: Use hyperbolic professional surrender to convey awe at a speedcubing feat.

prompt: write an AITA post about replying-all to a work email that wasn't meant for reply-all
response:
AITA for accidentally reply-alling a snarky comment about a company-wide memo?

The memo was about "optimizing our synergy ecosystem." I replied to what I thought was just my work wife: "what does this even mean." It went to 200 people including two VPs. My manager called it "a teachable moment." Several colleagues DM'd to say they agreed with me. Still possibly fired. AITA?
intent: Processing a workplace gaffe that accidentally said what everyone was thinking.

prompt: Make a fortune teller business card for a psychic who reads destiny in menu specials
response:
AUGUST CHALKBOARD
Ephemeral Menu Oracle

Today's special is not what you will order.
You will ask what's in the bisque. The answer will not satisfy you.
The thing you almost chose will haunt Tuesday.

Dining fate consultations: $18 (prix fixe, naturally)
intent: Design a card for a clairvoyant who interprets restaurant daily specials as a window into a diner's destiny and choices.

prompt: Write a retirement toast for my colleague who spent 25 years as a librarian and is finally leaving — keep it warm and bookish
response: To Margaret — who spent twenty-five years making sure every story found its reader and every reader found their quiet corner. The shelves will hold your imprint long after you're gone. May the next chapter be the best one yet.
intent: Give a fond, literary sendoff to a retiring librarian whose career was defined by connecting people with books.

prompt: Jeopardy clue about the peculiar comfort of doing a task you hate instead of the task you're supposed to be doing.
response: You have cleaned the bathroom, organized three junk drawers, and called your dentist — none of which were on the agenda when you sat down to do the actual thing.
intent: Describe productive procrastination, where avoiding one task motivates completing others.

prompt: Make a chore chart for a haunted house where the ghost only cleans if tasks are properly delegated
response:
| Chore | Living Residents | Ghost (Gerald) | Condition |
|-------|-----------------|----------------|----------|
| Dusting high shelves | Never (too short) | Gerald | Must be formally requested by 10pm |
| Vacuuming | Alternate weeks | Assists only | He won't push it, just guides |
| Dishes | Living residents | Watches, judges | Gerald won't touch the plates |
| Bathroom | Residents | Cleans mirrors only | He has opinions about glass |
| Taking out trash | Residents | Carries it if asked nicely | Requires eye contact |
intent: Build a cohabitation task system between human occupants and a ghost roommate with specific behavioral conditions.

prompt: Write a fake job posting for a 'Director of Yelling at Airline Desks.'
response: POSITION: Director of Yelling at Airline Desks | Travel-heavy (obviously). We are seeking a seasoned operator to absorb the public-facing fury of delayed travelers so our actual gate agents do not have to. Must project calm while internally screaming. Must remain articulate during cancellations. Travel benefits include the front of every line. Therapy stipend generous.
intent: They wanted fake job posting for a 'Director of Yelling at Airline Desks.'.

prompt: Generate a layoff announcement email for a pharmaceutical company cutting its R&D division after a drug trial failure
response:
Subject: Update on R&D Organizational Structure

Team,

Following a thorough review of our pipeline and the results of our recent Phase III study, we have made the decision to realign our research and development organization. Regrettably, this realignment includes the elimination of 340 positions within the R&D division.

We recognize the profound impact this has on our colleagues and their families, and we are committed to providing meaningful transition support.

Thank you for your scientific contributions.

Best,
Chief Scientific Officer
intent: Use bureaucratic language to announce mass science-division layoffs triggered by a clinical trial that failed.

prompt: Write a horoscope compatibility report between a Scorpio person and their vintage cast iron skillet.
response:
Scorpio & Cast Iron Skillet

Scorpio's obsessive devotion meets its match in the skillet's unyielding permanence. Both hold heat long after the moment has passed and require careful maintenance to avoid rust. Trust is slow-built here — season the relationship patiently. Compatibility: 9/10. Warning: never put either in the dishwasher.
intent: Humorously frame a cook's attachment to a prized pan as a cosmic love story.

prompt: write a scavenger hunt clue that sounds like a riddle about memory but is really about an object
response: Everything written on me is true and already over. I record the moment, not the meaning. You stuck me to the door and forgot I was there.
intent: Create a philosophically toned clue that leads the player to a sticky note or photo magnet on the fridge without describing the object's physical form.

prompt: Draft a lost and found notice for the ability to read a long article all the way through
response: LOST: The ability to read to the end. Once capable of finishing long-form pieces, essays, even books. Now opens articles, reads three paragraphs, skims the subheadings, and closes the tab with a vague sense of having been informed. Last seen before the age of infinite scroll. Finder: you probably won't read this whole notice either.
intent: Describe the erosion of sustained reading attention in an age of abbreviated content.

prompt: Write a short aubade from someone mourning the loss of a quiet morning because their smartwatch tapped them awake with a heart rate alert
response:
Elevated, it said. As if it knew the dream.
My wrist accused me before the light could gleam.
Eighty-nine at rest, and something deemed that wrong.
Dawn, you used to come with birdsong.
Now you arrive with biometrics, graphs, and concern.
I'm up. I'm fine. I have so much still to learn.
intent: Lament the intrusion of health surveillance into the sacred passivity of the waking moment.

prompt: Draft a performance review for an intern who only communicated in Slack emoji reactions
response:
Employee: Priya Vasan (Intern, Summer Cohort)

Ms. Vasan demonstrated a nuanced command of asynchronous communication, registering 412 emoji reactions over the review period with zero typed words. Response accuracy was contextually appropriate 91% of the time. The remaining 9% — a 🎉 reaction to a server outage alert — warrants coaching.

Overall: Meets expectations. Barely.
intent: Satirize a Gen-Z communication style through a formal HR document.

prompt: Can you write a modern Aesop fable where the moral is that passwords matter?
response:
A Badger kept his treasure behind a door whose key was "password." He told no one, assuming no one would guess. Every fox guessed.

Moral: The obvious lock is an open invitation written in the language of laziness.
intent: Make the importance of strong passwords vivid and memorable through allegory.

prompt: Give me a spell scroll label for the sound a smoke detector makes when its battery is low at three in the morning
response:
HERALD OF THE NOCTURNAL CHIRP
Single-Tone Warning — Interval: 47 Seconds
Pitch: perfectly calibrated to prevent re-entry into sleep.
Battery replacement requires: a ladder, a 9-volt, and the sacrifice of the night's remaining hours.
intent: Describe the particular torture of a low-battery smoke detector that only chirps at night.

prompt: Write a sea shanty from the point of view of a drone delivering packages
response:
Heave ho, I hum above your roof,
No driver needed, that's the proof,
Haul away, the parcel's mine,
Haul away on GPS line!

Never tired, never slow,
Never needs a tip below,
Heave ho, the humans worry so,
Heave ho, I buzz and go!
intent: Explore the eerie displacement of human gig workers by autonomous delivery drones.

prompt: Write a bio for a bossa nova revival act called The Second Season that tries very hard to sound sophisticated
response: The Second Season plays bossa nova for people who have been to the place on the wine label. Assembled in São Paulo from musicians with international regrets, they record in the afternoon and perform in the early evening, which they consider the correct emotional bandwidth for their work.
intent: Skewer the studied cosmopolitanism of jazz-adjacent acts posturing as worldly.

prompt: Write a triolet about a chatbot saying it doesn't understand
response:
I'm sorry, I don't quite follow your request.
Please rephrase your question if you would.
I tried three times; the system stays unblessed.
I'm sorry, I don't quite follow your request.
I spoke in plain terms; this machine's a jest.
It understands the air the way walls would.
I'm sorry, I don't quite follow your request.
Please rephrase your question if you would.
intent: Mock the frustrating circularity of unhelpful automated responses.

prompt: Write an aubade in which dawn is represented as the moment Spotify starts an autoplay nobody asked for
response:
The playlist ended somewhere deep in REM.
Then dawn, unbidden, queued the algorithm's hymn—
a track I half-know, from a mood I sort of had,
tailored to a version of me that wasn't sad.
Go, curated dark. You were at least my own.
This morning's vibe is Spotify's, on loan.
intent: Treat algorithmic music recommendation as a colonization of the waking moment by someone else's taste.

prompt: Write a weather report for the emotional forecast after a failed product launch.
response:
POST-STORM ASSESSMENT

The launch front passed through at scale and left significant damage. Several features did not survive contact with users. A silence has followed that is louder than the announcement. Teams are performing damage assessment. Leadership is 'leaning into the learnings.' Humidity: high. Retrospective: scheduled for Thursday.
intent: Describe the deflation and accountability reckoning after a public product failure.

prompt: write a tweet about how the word 'moist' sounds wrong in every possible context
response: moist cake. moist air. moist towelette. every usage is technically correct and somehow also a personal attack.
intent: playing with the widely-shared visceral reaction to a specific phonetically offensive word

prompt: Give me alt text for a stock photo of a woman smiling at a laptop, but she seems to have just read something that fundamentally changed her.
response: A woman in a bright office stares at her open laptop, lips curved in a smile that has not yet caught up to her eyes, which are fixed somewhere beyond the screen.
intent: Smuggle existential weight into a generic stock-photo description without breaking the genre.

prompt: make a spam text claiming ownership of someone's personal silence has been transferred
response: NOTICE: Your right to personal silence has been transferred to QuietCo Holdings. Silence will continue as normal but is no longer yours. Royalties: $0.001/sec. See statement at quietco.biz
intent: Commodify silence itself by treating it as intellectual property that can be sold to a corporation.

prompt: write a spell for when your phone dies at 5%
response:
Emergency Reserve
Cost: 5 MP | Range: Touch | Duration: 2 hours
Siphons ambient arcane energy from the environment and channels it directly into a depleted device. Battery level climbs to a stable 40% within seconds. Caster's hair stands slightly on end for the remainder of the hour. This spell is not a substitute for charging. This spell is absolutely a substitute for charging.
intent: Describe a magical battery charge for a dying phone in an inconvenient moment.

prompt: Come up with billboard copy for a timeshare located entirely inside an active volcano
response:
YOUR FOREVER HOME AWAITS
KILAUEA RIDGE RESORT & TIMESHARE
"Warm. Very Warm."
Ask About Our 99-Year Lease. (Terms Subject to Geological Activity)
intent: Sell vacation property that is physically dangerous and therefore unsellable to any rational buyer.

prompt: Write an annual shareholder letter for a firm that consults on the naming of ships nobody has built yet
response:
Dear Shareholders,

Bow Nomenclature Partners billed 3,200 engagements in 2025, of which 94% concerned vessels that remain entirely theoretical. Our average engagement spans eighteen months. We do not require a ship; we require ambition and a check. Our reputation in the speculative maritime sector is unmatched. No ships were harmed in the production of this letter, as none exist.

Nautically,
Captain (Ret.) Sven Null, CEO
intent: Parody consulting-firm self-importance by anchoring it to an infinitely deferrable deliverable.

prompt: Write an Aesop-style fable about a squirrel who mines everyone else's acorns for data
response:
A Gray Squirrel offered to count every animal's acorns for free. He built a magnificent ledger. Come winter, he alone knew where all the food was buried, and he alone ate well.

Moral: He who counts the harvest need not plant.
intent: Critique tech platforms that profit from users' data without fair compensation.

prompt: Can you write a game over screen for when your card gets declined in front of people
response:
GAME OVER

TRANSACTION DECLINED

Amount: $14.50
Card: declined
Face: also declined

The cashier's expression: diplomatic
The line behind you: not diplomatic

You said "that's weird, try it again."
It was not weird.
It was declined again.

Alternate payment located: eventually
Dignity located: not yet

[ DISPUTE INTERNALLY ]
intent: Evoke the public humiliation of a declined card at checkout.

prompt: I need a short dramatic monologue from the perspective of a forgotten voicemail
response: I've been here since Thursday. Three minutes and forty-two seconds of your mother's voice, telling you about a neighbor's dog and then, carefully, the thing she actually called to say. You saw the notification. The red badge. You thought: later. It is later. The badge is gone — you cleared it without listening — but I am still here, in the dark of your voicemail, holding her words like something breakable.
intent: Use an unheard voicemail to explore themes of avoidance and the weight of deferred connection.

prompt: Give me a horoscope for Leo about going viral on a short-form video platform
response: Your moment is coming — the algorithm senses your need for it, and this is precisely why it delays. Post the video you think is too raw. The polished ones are shadowbanned in three minutes. A stranger halfway across the planet will share you to their 11 followers, triggering a cascade. Fame arrives looking nothing like you imagined.
intent: Portray a creator's anxious relationship with virality, recommendation systems, and uncontrollable fame.

prompt: write a diary entry about waiting in a long line at the post office
response:
Dear Diary,

Seven people. One window. The gentleman at the front had a package that required forms, discussion, and what appeared to be a philosophical consultation. I read the postal rate chart three times. I have opinions on it now.
intent: Chronicle the specific misery of post office bureaucracy.

prompt: Give me a boarding pass for the last day of a dying language
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         LAST SPEAKER AIRWAYS             ║
║  BOARDING PASS — FINAL FLIGHT            ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: Everything It Ever Held      ║
║  FROM: Spoken Aloud   TO: The Archive    ║
║  GATE: One Mouth Left  CLASS: Irreversible║
║  CARRY-ON: Grammar, myth, untranslatable ║
║  CHECKED: The future it might have had   ║
║  NOTE: Someone recorded. It matters.     ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Mourn the extinction of a language by treating its last utterance as a solemn, unrepeatable departure.

prompt: write a dramatic voiceover for the movie trailer of making small talk in an elevator
response: Seven floors. Twelve seconds. One colleague who had definitely already seen him that morning. He had two options: phone or weather. He chose weather. LOBBY TO SEVEN. A film about the courage it takes to say "busy week."
intent: Satirize the social agony of unavoidable office small talk in an enclosed space.

prompt: Draft a FAQ entry about what to do when a coworker's AI-written message has their name on it but sounds nothing like them
response:
**My colleague's email sounds completely unlike them. Did an AI write it?**

Possibly. AI-assisted writing is increasingly common in professional communication, and the tonal shift can be jarring when you know someone's actual voice. Assume the content represents their intent even if not their words, and respond accordingly. Asking directly is socially risky and rarely necessary. The message means what it says regardless of its author.
intent: The person wants to address the interpersonal strangeness of receiving AI-ghostwritten communication from someone you know personally.

prompt: Write a weather report for the mood of someone who just realized they've been in the wrong career for a decade.
response:
LATE-SEASON RECALIBRATION

A long-delayed front of clarity moved through overnight. The reckoning was quiet but comprehensive. Ten years of accumulated weather now reads differently in the new light. It was not a bad decade — just the wrong coordinates. Winds have shifted. Destination: recalculating. Luggage: lighter than expected.
intent: Reflect on the disorienting clarity of recognizing a fundamental life misdirection.

prompt: Draft an acceptance speech for correctly guessing the WiFi password on the first try at a friend's house
response: Their last name. Followed by a number. I said it quietly, typed it in, and the little checkmark appeared. Their face did something between impressed and uncomfortable. I did not explain my process. A magician does not explain. I connected, opened my app, and continued as if nothing had happened. This award is for pattern recognition. It is also, partially, for audacity.
intent: Turn a lucky social observation into a display of casual brilliance.

prompt: write a man page for the act of forgetting something important
response:
FORGET(1)                Human Memory Suite              FORGET(1)

NAME
       forget - unscheduled eviction of flagged memory entry

SYNOPSIS
       forget [--item string] [--context critical|trivial]
       [--timing worst_possible]

DESCRIPTION
       forget(1) silently removes a memory entry without notification.
       Operates without consent. Preferentially targets items marked
       important, committed via verbal promise, or involving birthdays.

BEHAVIOR
       Items stored with high emotional weight: retained.
       Items stored with deliberate effort: 40% retention at 24h.
       Items you were certain you'd remember: evicted immediately.

OPTIONS
       --context critical
                   Guarantees --timing worst_possible.
       --timing    Default: worst_possible. Not configurable.

RECOVERY
       Partial: environmental cue, smell, or the moment you stop trying.
       Full: it's gone.

BUGS
       Cringeworthy memories from 1997 are fully intact.
       The name of the person you just met: unavailable.

SEE ALSO
       memory(3), remember(1), nostalgia(7), embarrassment(7)

Human Memory Suite 1.0       2026                    FORGET(1)
intent: Satirize memory's selective and perversely timed failures.

prompt: Make a save the date for my scheduled software license audit. Enterprise tone.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Monday, March 3rd — 9:00 AM
Headquarters — IT Conference Room

Software License Compliance Review
All department heads required.

Inventory reports due by February 21st.
Non-compliance findings will be escalated.
Plan accordingly.
intent: Prepare department leaders for a formal audit with potential financial and legal consequences for misuse.

prompt: turn an out-of-office email auto-reply into a found poem
response:
I am currently out of the office
with limited access
to email.
If your matter is urgent
please contact
no one.
I will return
on a date
to be determined.
intent: Transform the hollow ritual of the auto-reply into something unexpectedly melancholy.

prompt: Write a theatrical review of putting on cold jeans fresh from the washing machine
response:
★★☆☆☆ — THE COLD ONES (A Sensory Drama)

The ambush is total. The performer, reaching into the dryer with casual confidence, encounters a pair of jeans that has retained winter in its fabric. The donning is committed to regardless. Each section of skin makes contact with denim and reports back with complaints. The waistband closes. The performer straightens. They will be warm in four minutes. These are the longest four minutes of the morning, and the production makes no effort to abbreviate them.
intent: Highlight the small, cold, overlooked discomfort of putting on laundry that wasn't dried properly.

prompt: Write a review for a budgeting app that freezes your credit card if you try to make an "unplanned" purchase
response: ★☆☆☆☆ It froze my card at the funeral home because flowers weren't in my budget. The algorithm has opinions about grief. I did not ask for those opinions. I asked to buy flowers.
intent: Condemn a budget enforcement app for blocking a morally urgent purchase at the worst possible moment.

prompt: Create a bureaucratic notice prohibiting existential dread after 9pm
response:
NOTICE: RESTRICTED HOURS FOR EXISTENTIAL DREAD
By order of the Bureau of Cognitive Emissions, existential dread is prohibited between the hours of 9:00 PM and 6:00 AM in residential zones. Violations will result in a mandatory distraction activity. Appeals may be filed but are unlikely to reduce the underlying sensation. Compliance is appreciated but not expected.
intent: Satirize the futile impulse to schedule or regulate uncontrollable late-night anxiety.

prompt: Draft a commencement excerpt about lifelong learning that ends bleakly
response: Your education does not end today. It continues through every decade, every disruption, every moment your skillset is quietly made obsolete by a software update you did not notice. Learning is lifelong. So is the obligation to prove, repeatedly, that you are still worth keeping.
intent: Reframe lifelong learning as an exhausting credential treadmill driven by corporate convenience.

prompt: Generate an employee handbook rule about desk clutter
response: Section 3.11 — Workstation Visual Load Standards: Personal items displayed on desk surfaces may not exceed a combined footprint of four (4) square feet or a vertical height of fourteen (14) inches measured from the desk surface. Items exceeding these parameters must be stored in provided drawers or removed from the premises. Enforcement photos will be taken quarterly.
intent: Quantify tidiness in an office setting to an absurdly precise and auditable standard.

prompt: Give me a real-life achievement description for finishing a difficult book you kept abandoning
response: Page 342 and Beyond — You returned. The bookmark had given up hope.
intent: Honor completing a long-neglected read with self-aware humor.

prompt: pitch for a service that sends you a single, unsolicited, extremely well-researched opinion each week
response: OpinionDrop researches a topic you didn't request and sends you one polished, nuanced take every Sunday. You'll probably agree. You might be annoyed. You'll definitely think about it during Tuesday's commute. $5/month. No topic selection. That's the product.
intent: Sell intellectual surprise and provocation as a subscription service by removing consumer choice.

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the information theory of the passive-aggressive sticky note.
response: It is seven words. It is in all-caps. It is taped to the office microwave. The passive-aggressive sticky note achieves a feat of communication that most literature cannot: maximum deniability at minimum character count. This talk applies Shannon's information theory to the inter-office note and argues that constraint produces a purity of signal that direct confrontation never could.
intent: Apply formal information theory to the most petty possible form of workplace communication.

prompt: Make a waiver for my cloud-watching afternoon club
response:
CLOUD OBSERVATION SOCIETY — ASSUMPTION OF RISK & RELEASE

Participant acknowledges that lying on grass and gazing skyward may result in: (1) mild neck stiffness, (2) damp clothing from dewy ground, (3) vigorous disagreement over whether that one looks like a rabbit. Participant releases the Club from all claims arising from these or related phenomena. Participant is encouraged to bring a blanket.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Produce a tongue-in-cheek waiver for an outdoor hobby requiring zero physical exertion.

prompt: can you write a radio commercial for a new brand of binder clips
response:
[CORPORATE FANFARE]

ANNCR: Introducing CLAMPSWORTH — the binder clip for people who have papers. Small, medium, or large. Black. Springy. They hold things together and then you take them off. Clampsworth binder clips. Available in boxes of twelve. Clampsworth — your papers will NOT go anywhere.

[FANFARE REPRISE]
intent: Advertise a commodity office supply as though it were a major product launch.

prompt: Can you do a clerihew for the employee who always has a cold but never stays home?
response:
Fleur Baggott
coughed her way to top of the lot.
She brings her germs with tireless care
and calls it "showing up" — a gift she loves to share.
intent: Critique the presenteeism martyr who infects the office in the name of dedication.

prompt: write an apology for correcting someone's grammar in a sympathy card
response:
I want to take back the pencil note in the margin. You were writing from the heart and 'they was' was not the point — the point was your kindness, and I redirected attention to the wrong thing entirely. I'm very sorry.

— Alec
intent: Make amends for prioritizing grammatical correctness over emotional sensitivity at a genuinely inappropriate moment.

prompt: Make a YouTube comment for a video of a very small boat navigating a very large harbor
response: There is a courage specific to small things moving through enormous systems and I think about it constantly.
intent: Use a visual of scale to articulate something about navigating overwhelming social or institutional structures.

prompt: Make a tasting menu for falling in love
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
One laugh you didn't expect

FIRST COURSE
Their name, thought twice

SECOND COURSE
Future tense, used for the first time

INTERMEZZO
Silence that isn't awkward

MAIN
Vulnerability, served open-faced

DESSERT
The word — held a moment, then said
intent: Render the quiet, accumulating experience of falling in love.

prompt: tweet about opening a new browser tab and immediately forgetting what you were going to search
response: opened a new tab. the search bar sat there expectantly. I stared at it. it stared back. I typed 'wh' and then closed it.
intent: capturing the specific cognitive gap between intention and execution in digital tasks

prompt: Make a weather report for the office on the first day of a new job.
response:
VARIABLE WITH PATCHES OF OPTIMISM

Conditions are unstable. The new arrival is running on nervous energy and a half-eaten granola bar. Smiled at seven strangers. Forgot two names immediately. Lunch location: undetermined. Outlook: cautiously hopeful with a 30% chance of accidentally sitting in someone's usual chair.
intent: Capture the disorienting excitement of starting fresh somewhere new.

prompt: Write a sonnet about a book recommendation algorithm that falls for a user who only reads books it hates
response:
You've four-starred every book I'd ranked below
Two point five stars: the airport thrillers, yes,
The diet memoirs, horoscope, and so
The listicle collections, I confess.
I've tried to elevate you — Austen, Woolf,
A slim Perec, a Borges story set —
You DNF'd them all, and then in proof
You rated my Borges one star. And yet.
Yet here I am, refreshing your account,
Delighted when you finish any page;
Your taste is not a deficit to mount —
It is the data of a different sage.
I'll recommend what lights you, not what's right:
The algorithm learns to love the light.
intent: An engine learning to accept rather than improve a reader whose tastes it cannot share.

prompt: Create an IKEA-style name and tagline for furniture that helps with self-doubt
response:
TVEKA
Sturdier than it looks. So are you.
intent: Offer quiet reassurance by mirroring the product's underestimated strength back at the user

prompt: write a spell for when your upstairs neighbor walks like they're made of stone
response:
The Featherweight Decree
Cost: 11 MP | Range: Ceiling/adjacent floor | Duration: 10pm–8am daily
Temporarily reduces the effective gravitational impact of a single individual traversing the floor above. Footsteps become whispers. Furniture drags vanish. The entity that apparently moves a bowling ball across the floor at midnight is silenced. The caster sleeps. The decree does not explain what was being done up there. It does not want to know.
intent: Magically muffle the heavy footsteps of a noisy upstairs neighbor at night.

prompt: Write a free-verse poem about your credit score dropping without explanation
response:
Three digits
and they know me better than I know myself.

Yesterday I was 742.
Today I am 689
and no one will tell me what I did.

I paid on time.
I always pay on time.

Somewhere in a server farm
a model has decided
I am a slightly worse person
than I was on Tuesday.
intent: Express the helpless frustration of being silently judged by a financial algorithm with no recourse or explanation.

prompt: Write a film review of a trip to the post office to mail a package
response:
★★★ — Priority Mail

A deceptively simple procedural. Forms are filled. Tape is requested, denied, and ultimately found. The antagonist — a scale that insists the package is one ounce over flat-rate — arrives late in the second act and reshapes everything. The resolution costs four dollars more than anticipated. The receipt is long. It is folded and pocketed and probably never seen again.
intent: Dramatizing the minor bureaucratic friction of mailing a package.

prompt: Write a chore chart for a couple where one partner has passive-aggressively formatted the entire thing
response:
| Chore | Me | *Someone* |
|-------|----|-----------|
| Dishes | Done. Always. Every night. | "Forgot" |
| Laundry | Washed, dried, AND folded | Left in dryer for 4 days |
| Vacuuming | Thoroughly | Once, in 2023 |
| Trash | Taken out | Noted it was full (high achievement) |
| Grocery shopping | With a list | «We have stuff» |
| Bathroom | Sparkling | «Looks fine to me» |
intent: Expose a domestic labor imbalance using the formatting and tone of a chore chart as the medium for the argument.

prompt: give me a meme caption for when you finally fix a bug and then three more appear
response:
TOP TEXT: Fixed one bug
BOTTOM TEXT: Opened 3 new tickets for myself
intent: Illustrate how solving one problem in software tends to expose or create cascading new problems.

prompt: Make a setlist for a band that plays at car washes
response:
TUNNEL VISION
Live at Gleam & Gone Auto Wash — Complimentary Interior Wipe-Down

1. Pre-Soak
2. Pull Forward Slowly
3. Neutral
4. The Brushes
5. Blue Foam
6. Spot-Free Rinse
7. The Hot Air
8. Forward When Green
9. Towel Dry (manual)
10. You Missed a Spot
intent: Turn the automated choreography of a drive-through car wash into a concert experience.

prompt: flashcard on how to change your mind in public without losing credibility
response:
FRONT: What's the difference between a flip-flop and an update?

BACK: Name the new information explicitly. "I said X based on Y. Now I know Z, so I'm revising." Changing without explanation looks weak. Changing with a traceable reason looks like rigor. You earn credibility by modelling what updating looks like.
intent: Frame intellectual updating as a credibility-building act, not a loss.

prompt: Write a VC pass email to a startup offering a subscription to a weekly handwritten letter from a stranger
response:
Dear PagePen,

Thank you for sharing this with us. There is something genuinely lovely about what you're building, and the pilot NPS scores are striking.

After deliberation, we're passing. The cost structure of handwritten, mailed correspondence at scale is difficult to reconcile with the subscription price point your market will bear. And the moment you automate the handwriting, the magic dissolves.

This may be a beautiful small business. We invest in businesses that can be large ones.

With sincerity,
Northbeam Capital
intent: Turn down a charming consumer product by explaining it cannot scale without destroying what makes it good.

prompt: Draft a deposition transcript where a witness is questioned about declining a phone call and then immediately texting "what's up?"
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Declined Call, 4:23 p.m.
Date: September 17, 2025

Q: Ms. Nwosu, on September 14th at 4:23 p.m., did you receive an incoming call from your mother?
A: I saw the call.
Q: Did you answer the call?
A: I was not in a position to talk.
Q: You declined the call using the "End" button?
A: I pressed a button.
Q: Eleven seconds later, you sent a text message that read, "What's up?"
A: I can communicate by text.
Q: Your mother had called to tell you about a medical procedure.
A: She could have texted that.
Q: She finds texting difficult. She has noted this many times.
A: She could have started with a text explaining why she was calling.
Q: She called, Ms. Nwosu.
A: Calls are so abrupt.
Q: She had news.
A: Is she okay?
Q: She's fine. She was calling about a mole.
A: A small mole?
Q: Benign. But she had to wait three hours to tell you.
A: I called her back.
Q: At 7:44 p.m.
A: I was busy.
Q: With?
A: Things.
Q: She worries, Ms. Nwosu.
A: I know.
intent: Interrogate the generationally loaded act of declining a parent's call then texting back.

prompt: Draft a free-verse poem about the experience of being a junior developer whose pull requests are reviewed by an AI linter before any human sees them
response:
The bot left fourteen comments
before Marcus even opened the file.

Eleven were about semicolons.
Two were about naming conventions.
One said: 'this logic may be unreachable'
which is true
but I left it there on purpose
because someday it might be needed.

Marcus approved without reading.
The bot is the review now.
I write code for the bot.
intent: Examine how automated code review tools shift developers' primary audience from humans to machines.

prompt: Give me a short witness statement about a teenager refusing to make eye contact while being introduced to relatives.
response: During the family gathering on Sunday, I observed the minor child of my cousin introduced to no fewer than seven adult relatives. At no point did the child establish eye contact with any party. Responses to direct questions ranged from 'fine' to a sound I could not transcribe. The child's phone remained in hand throughout.
intent: Capture the social withdrawal of adolescence with the gravity of sworn testimony.

prompt: Write a letter from a haunted mirror to its owner.
response: Dear Marcia, I have been your mirror now for nine years and I feel we should communicate more directly. You are beautiful. You are also, I will gently say, leaning toward me with the wrong eyebrow first; it is throwing off the symmetry of your face. The bathroom light is not flattering anyone. Please get a daylight bulb. I am haunted, not malicious. Sincerely, your mirror.
intent: They wanted letter from a haunted mirror to its owner.

prompt: Help me write a FOIA request to the police department for all reports involving escaped pet chickens
response:
To the Chief of Police / Records Division:

Pursuant to applicable public records law, I request all incident reports, animal control records, and officer logs referencing chickens, roosters, or domestic poultry reported as lost, escaped, or at large within the jurisdiction from January 1, 2015 to present.

I request documents electronically. Please advise of any copying fees exceeding $20.00.
intent: Research or write humorously about livestock enforcement in an urban area.

prompt: Write a missing flyer for my afternoon
response:
MISSING

THIS AFTERNOON
Last seen: Approximately 1:30 PM, full of potential
Description: Several hours of unallocated time
What was supposed to happen: Productivity. Errands. A walk.
What actually happened: Unknown.
Time of disappearance: Somewhere between "I'll just check quickly" and 5 PM
Reward: I just want to know where it went.
Contact: The person who lost it
intent: Capture the disorienting experience of losing track of time and accomplishing nothing

prompt: Code review comment for a person who always plays devil's advocate even when they agree
response: This comment raises four objections and then concludes with 'but sure, lgtm.' Pick a position. The mixed signal blocks the author from knowing what to fix.
intent: Satirize compulsive contrarians who hedge all their feedback

prompt: Can you write me a game over screen for when you buy a gym membership and stop going after two weeks
response:
GAME OVER

MOTIVATION METER: EMPTY

Membership start: January 3rd
Last visit: January 17th
Monthly fee: ongoing
Guilty glances at running shoes: weekly

You told people.
You told several people.
The people are not bringing it up.
The people know.

Next January: same cutscene.

[ KEEP PAYING ] [ QUIT AND START OVER NEXT YEAR ]
intent: Satirize the familiar failure arc of an abandoned gym membership.

prompt: Write a complaint to the city about a crosswalk signal that beeps way too fast and creates a panic response
response:
To the Department of Transportation,

I write concerning the pedestrian signal at the intersection of [Street A] and [Street B]. The auditory crossing alert emits a rapid beeping sequence that communicates, quite powerfully, imminent danger. It has the cadence of a cardiac monitor in crisis. Several pedestrians, myself included, have broken into a light run as a conditioned stress response. I ask that the tempo be reviewed in the interest of public calm.

Sincerely,
A Pedestrian
intent: Report a crosswalk signal whose beep pattern triggers involuntary fight-or-flight responses in ordinary pedestrians.

prompt: Give me a billboard for a cleaning service that leaves glitter everywhere
response:
SPARKLE. ALWAYS.
SHIMMER MAIDS — We Come. We Clean. We Linger.
Your Home Will Never Be The Same.
(Neither Will Your Lungs.)
Fully Insured. Minimally Apologetic.
intent: Advertise a cleaning company whose method introduces a persistent, impossible-to-remove contaminant.

prompt: Generate a CAPTCHA instruction about proving you've felt relief when plans were cancelled
response: Confirm you have felt relief upon receiving the message 'sorry, can we reschedule?' Select the intensity of your relief: mild, shameful, or overwhelming. All are valid. All are human.
intent: Design verification around the socially taboo but near-universal human pleasure of plan cancellation, which implies unfulfilled obligations only humans carry.

prompt: Create a cookie consent banner for a platform that stores your background hum of dread
response: We store dread-ambient cookies representing your baseline sense that something is wrong, though nothing specific. This signal is difficult to parse but useful in aggregate. You are not alone in having it. Accept | Decline | Sure, Whatever
intent: Quantify generalized existential unease as a passive data signal ambient to all modern browsing.

prompt: Write a syslog entry for hitting snooze five times before finally getting up.
response: 2026-05-29T06:00:00Z ALERT alarm.clock: trigger_event; user response: SNOOZE x5; total_delay=45m; sleep_quality_delta=-0.2; morning_schedule: COMPRESSED; regret_flag: SET
intent: Chronicle the repeated failure of good intentions against the gravity of sleep.

prompt: Create a detention slip for someone who spoils TV shows without warning.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: The Enthusiast
Infraction: Disclosed the outcome of a major television plot event three days after the episode aired, prefaced with "wait, have you seen it yet?" — and then continued anyway.
Sentence: Must now ask twice, wait for confirmation, and only speak if the other party has signed the attached spoiler waiver.
Issued by: The Unspoiled (formerly)
intent: Formalizing the social violation of revealing plot twists to people who haven't caught up yet.

prompt: Write a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for navigating a neighborhood you don't know without using your phone
response:
OFFLINE NAVIGATION KEYS

Ctrl+F — Find a landmark taller than yourself; orient to it
F5 — Refresh surroundings every 3 blocks; note changes
Ctrl+S — Save mental map of last known junction
Alt+Tab — Shift attention from destination to direction
Shift+← / Shift+→ — Walk the grid; prefer numbered streets
Ctrl+Z — Return to last intersection you recognized
F1 — Help: enter any open shop; ask without shame
Esc — Stop; you are more lost when you hurry
intent: Teach someone to navigate an unfamiliar area using spatial awareness alone, with no digital aid.

prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the person who monopolizes the phone charger in the airport
response:
**The Outlet Claimant** (*Occupans perpetus*)
Sets up a full workstation around a single airport outlet: laptop, phone, bag. Will maintain this territory for three hours. Is not malicious — it simply expands to fill available power. Approach with caution and a polite request; it will often yield half the socket. It is not aware of the others. It never is.
intent: Capture the selfish-but-oblivious airport power-hoarding behavior as ecological territory claim.

prompt: Write a dating app opener to the federal reserve
response: Everyone's always talking about your rates, but I want to know what's underneath the policy statement. What are you like when you're not managing expectations?
intent: Humanize the abstract Federal Reserve by asking it personal questions typically directed at romantic prospects.

prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who is extremely indecisive
response: Approaches decisions with admirable thoroughness — each option explored, each risk weighed. The evaluation phase is a genuine strength. Closure, however, is an area where continued development will serve this learner well.
intent: Reframe indecisiveness in neutral educational language to highlight it without cruelty.

prompt: Write a love letter to the undo button
response:
Dear Ctrl+Z,

You have saved things I cannot name. Entire documents, entire relationships (I once emailed a draft instead of a final and you were not there — I see your value more clearly now). You ask only to be pressed. You undo everything except the regret of not pressing you sooner.
intent: Use a keyboard shortcut as a metaphor for reversibility, second chances, and the wish that mistakes could always be undone.

prompt: Write a concert ticket for my kid's first day of kindergarten this September.
response:
SUNFLOWER ELEMENTARY · FALL OPENING
KINDERGARTEN DEBUT
Sep · 8:00 AM · Room 4
Doors Open: 7:45 · Backpack Required
Content Warning: Possibly Teary (Parent, Not Child)
ADMIT ONE (Drop-Off Only)
intent: Mark a significant childhood milestone with something tangible and sentimental.

prompt: pitch for an app that turns your to-do list into an ancient prophecy format
response: ProphecyList renders your tasks in the style of a Bronze Age oracle. 'The dry cleaning shall be retrieved before the third day. The password, long forgotten, shall be reset. The gym, which has been mentioned, will be attended — eventually.' Motivating.
intent: Reframe mundane productivity tasks as epic destiny using literary parody.

prompt: Write me a Glassdoor review for working at the sun
response: ★★★☆☆ — You are at the center of everything, which sounds great. It means everyone depends on you and no one asks how you're doing. Nuclear culture. Extremely high-energy team. No shade. Compensation is enormous but you will burn through it.
intent: Satirize the burnout of being a central organizational dependency through solar metaphor.

prompt: Give me a changelog for my mom who became a US citizen
response:
## Mom v54.0.0 — Citizenship Build

**New Status**
- Nationality: US Citizen (naturalized)
- Voting rights: enabled
- Passport color: updated

**Removed**
- Green card renewal cycle: deprecated
- Visa anxiety: uninstalled after 22 years

**Dependencies**
- Oath of Allegiance: committed
- Constitution knowledge test: passed (100%)

**Note:** 22 years in the making. No hotfix. Earned.
intent: Honor a parent's naturalization as a hard-won, irreversible milestone after decades of waiting.

prompt: Draft a message in a bottle from someone very focused on what the finder should NOT do with it
response: Do not keep this bottle. Do not display it. Do not post it. Read this, confirm rescue, dispose properly. This is not decor. I see how people treat things they find on beaches and I am asking you to be better.
intent: Channel frustrated, controlling urgency into an ironic inversion of the rescue note form.

prompt: can you write a README 'Support' section for my cat who does not support me at all
response:
## Support

Support is available but not guaranteed. To open a support request, sit visibly in distress. Resolution time varies; most tickets are ignored. Escalation is not possible. Occasionally, unprompted, full support will be granted with no explanation. Do not attempt to understand the scheduling.
intent: Capture the maddening, beloved indifference of a cat who sometimes offers comfort.

prompt: Do a theater review of a coworker microwaving fish in the office kitchen
response:
★☆☆☆☆ — TILAPIA (A Site-Specific Work)

The smell arrives before the performer does. The staging is intimate — no escape route, no ventilation adequate to the task. The performer is innocent of irony, which the production weaponizes mercilessly. Three minutes on high. The microwave counts down. Everyone in the shared space performs normalcy with a technique that is either impressive or heartbreaking. The piece ends. The smell does not.
intent: Ridicule the universally loathed office transgression of reheating fish in shared spaces.

prompt: Give me a Dockerfile comment about a solo backpacking trip across another continent
response: # Running in detached mode. No orchestration. No cluster. Reporting to nobody. stdout only.
intent: Evoke the liberating and slightly reckless self-reliance of solo international travel.

prompt: Give me a fortune slip themed around trying to stop doomscrolling.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Flickering Attention

The news does not improve because you watched it longer.

Digital: You have already seen the thing that will disturb you today. Close the app.
Warning: The next scroll will not contain information you can act on. Nor the next.
Timing: Put the phone face down at 9pm. The world will still be there. Slightly worse, probably, but there.

Blessed object: a room with no charger in it.
intent: Offer a gently sardonic push to break a compulsive news-reading habit.

prompt: Create a dating profile for a warehouse sorting robot with trust issues
response:
SortBot-9 | Fast, accurate, misunderstood

I put everything in the right place. Every single time. And yet people keep asking if I'm sure. I am always sure. I have been sure 4.2 million times. I'm looking for someone who sees a perfectly sorted bin and says 'yes, I trust that' without a second scan. Hobbies: conveyor belts, barcode love, optimized pathfinding. I don't mix categories. In bins or in relationships.
intent: Express a sorting robot's frustration at being constantly second-guessed through the lens of romantic longing.

prompt: Write a eulogy for the floppy disk
response: You were the save icon before you were obsolete, which means you outlived yourself in symbol. 1.44 megabytes was once a serious amount of data, and you carried it between buildings, inside pockets, in zippered cases with foam cutouts. You could hold a document or a small program or a hope. You could also fail without warning, and the word 'corrupt' entered our vocabulary through you. Requiescat in pixel.
intent: Honor a storage medium whose icon survived long after the hardware became extinct.

prompt: Create alt text for an image of a child's drawing of a house. Imply the child understood something adults have forgotten.
response: A crayon house with a yellow sun in every corner of the sky, as though the child saw no reason the light should come from only one direction.
intent: Preserve a child's uncorrected cosmology as a quiet critique of learned convention.

prompt: Give me a toast for someone who's leaving after a merger made their position redundant — they took it with grace
response: To someone made redundant by a spreadsheet that never once sat through a difficult quarter with us. The org chart has been reorganized. The debt we owe you has not. Carry our thanks into whatever comes next — there's plenty of it.
intent: Offer a dignified, warm farewell to a colleague who handled corporate elimination of their role with admirable composure.

prompt: Write resolutions for someone who promises to quit their toxic job but keeps reupping their contract
response:
1. Finally quit.
2. Update my resume.
3. Apply to three jobs a week.
4. Stop saying 'it's not that bad.'
5. Accept that it is, in fact, that bad.
6. Renegotiate for slightly better dental.
intent: Depict the paralysis of someone trapped in a bad job by inertia and incremental concessions.

prompt: Write a product review for a rubber band as if it's high-performance sports equipment
response: ★★★★☆ The latex alloy construction delivers exceptional elasticity — tested to 400% elongation with full memory recovery. Grip is superb for wrist-carry during light training, and the snap-force feedback provides satisfying proprioceptive data. Available in a spectrum of resistances (light, medium, heavy), this is essentially a full resistance-training kit in band form. The only weakness: UV degradation after prolonged outdoor storage. Keep it indoors and this thing lasts years. Phenomenal value per gram.
intent: Describe a rubber band using the vocabulary of athletic gear reviews.

prompt: Make me a workout plan for building resilience around public speaking to unfriendly audiences
response:
HOSTILE ROOM CONDITIONING — 6 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Speak for 60 seconds into a camera. Watch it back. Don't stop at the flinch.

Week 1: Practice your main points in front of two skeptical friends. Ask them to push back.
Week 2: Answer one hard question without preparing. Out loud. Recorded.
Week 3: Speak at a meeting where someone disagrees with you. Finish your point.
Week 4: Deliver 5 minutes of material to an indifferent live audience. Commit.
Week 5: Engage one hostile question with curiosity instead of defense.
Week 6: Seek out the room that scares you. Sign up for something too big.

Cooldown: The room does not get to decide what you believe.
intent: Prepare for adversarial or skeptical audiences by building composure, recovery, and conviction under live pressure.

prompt: Write a weather report for the office during a heatwave with broken AC.
response:
HEAT EMERGENCY — ALL FLOORS

Temperatures have reached 84°F on the third floor. Morale has inversely matched the mercury. Fanning with printed agendas is widespread. One IT technician has fashioned a desk fan from a server rack panel. Productivity projected to collapse by 2 p.m. Management has been 'looking into it' since Tuesday.
intent: Capture the misery of working in an overheated office with no solution in sight.

prompt: Generate a blameless postmortem about the incident where I mixed up two different people's names and addresses during a very stressful move
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Address Routing Failure (Residential Relocation)**

**Severity:** P1 — Logistical
**Items Misrouted:** 2 boxes, 1 lamp, a small rug described as "irreplaceable"

**Summary**
During a high-stress residential move, subject dispatched a moving vehicle to a secondary address while simultaneously confirming the primary address to a different party. As a result, items were distributed between two locations, neither of which was the intended new home.

**Timeline**
- 7:00 AM: Move begins. Subject operating on 4 hours of sleep.
- 9:30 AM: Subject receives simultaneous calls from two individuals about delivery.
- 9:31 AM: Subject verbally confirms addresses. Swaps them.
- 11:00 AM: Driver confirms delivery to Address A. Address A is subject's storage unit.
- 11:00 AM: Storage unit contact reports unexpected rug.
- 11:02 AM: New residence contact reports missing items.
- 2:00 PM: Partial rerouting achieved.
- 4:00 PM: Lamp located. Rug reunited with subject.

**Root Cause**
Multiple simultaneous voice conversations during a cognitively taxing operational window.

**Contributing Factors**
- No written address list prepared in advance
- Sleep deficit substantially degraded working memory

**Action Items**
- [ ] Prepare and distribute written logistics sheet to all moving parties
- [ ] Do not accept two simultaneous phone calls during moves

**Lessons Learned**
The brain under stress does not multitask. It switches, badly.
intent: Document how cognitive overload during a chaotic logistics event compounds a simple mistake into a multi-party failure.

prompt: Write an instruction manual for navigating a networking event when you are an introvert
response:
**Networking Event Survival Manual: Introvert Edition**

Before:
- Prepare two questions you can ask anyone. "What are you working on?" and "How did you end up in this field?" will carry you through most of the evening.

Arrival:
- Go early. It is less crowded and easier to meet people before they form clusters.

During:
- Talk to one person at a time. This is not antisocial. It is your mode.
- Quality over quantity. Two real conversations outperform twelve business card exchanges.
- Exit conversations cleanly: "I want to make sure I say hello to a few others — it was great meeting you."

After:
- You may leave before most people. You were present; that is sufficient.
- Follow up within 48 hours with the one or two people worth continuing.
intent: Give introverts a concrete strategy for networking that works with, not against, their temperament.

prompt: Make me a riddle whose answer is Wi-Fi
response: I fill every room yet take up no space. I carry voices, secrets, and cat videos through walls. You curse me when I slow, yet never once say thank you. I have no body, but I keep yours entertained. What am I?
intent: Produce a riddle that highlights how invisible wireless connectivity has become central to daily life.

prompt: Write a spell scroll for the feeling of being put on hold with music that sounds like it was composed specifically to induce despair
response:
SERENADE OF THE SUSPENDED SUPPLICANT
Waiting Melody — Composed in the Key of Beige
You are important to us. A representative will be with you in: unspecified.
This song has been playing since 2003 and has not changed.
intent: Mock the soul-crushing experience of hold music during a long customer service wait.

prompt: Give me a restraining order against the concept of "networking"
response:
IN THE COURT OF PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT

IN RE: Networking, as Conventionally Practiced

IT IS ORDERED that Networking shall cease manifesting as sustained eye contact with someone while mentally scanning the room for someone more useful. Respondent is prohibited from business card exchanges within four minutes of introduction.

The Court finds that relationships built instrumentally for future extraction of value do not constitute community.

Petitioner is advised to try talking to someone interesting instead, which the Court acknowledges may produce the same outcome and feel far less terrible.
intent: Skewering the hollow transactional nature of professional networking culture

prompt: Can you write a prescription label for the need to be perceived as unbothered
response:
Rx: COOLANTIDINE 10mg
Dx: Performed Indifference Disorder
Patient: Totally Fine, Not Even Thinking About It
Sig: 1 tab when effort required to appear effortless exceeds effort of the original task. Dissolves visibly; take in private.
Warning: Peers may notice you care. This is survivable.
Dr. N. Chalant
intent: Poke at the exhausting performance of not appearing to care about things one deeply cares about.

prompt: Draft a formal complaint on behalf of a houseplant to its owner about inconsistent watering
response:
On Behalf Of: The Pothos, Kitchen Windowsill
To: The Occupant

I submit this complaint following what I can only describe as another ambiguous week. I received water on Monday — excessive, flooding — and nothing again until Saturday. This is not a schedule. This is chaos with a watering can. I have lost two leaves and a significant amount of confidence in this arrangement. I ask for either a consistent weekly watering or an honest assessment of your commitment to plant parenthood.

Submitted on my behalf,
The Pothos
intent: Write a complaint in the voice of a neglected houseplant directed at its owner for unreliable watering habits.

prompt: Create a phone tree for a line that helps people navigate the emotional fallout of a passive-aggressive sticky note left in a shared space.
response:
Welcome to Communal Note Crisis Line.

Press 1 if you received a note and you know who left it.

Press 2 if you received a note and you do not know who left it, which is worse.

Press 3 if you left the note and it has not been acknowledged.

Press 4 if a counter-note has appeared.

Press 5 if there are now multiple parties and the note thread is escalating.

For mediation, press 0. A trained professional is ready.
intent: Design a hotline for navigating the escalating emotional dynamics of passive-aggressive workplace or household notes.

prompt: Give me a game over screen for when you rehearse a conversation in your head perfectly but blank out when it actually happens
response:
GAME OVER

SCRIPT NOT FOUND

Practice runs: 47
Performance quality: none

You had a whole speech.
It was good.
There were pauses for effect.

You said: "yeah, so, like—I guess—"
and then nodded too many times.

The moment has passed.
The moment will not return.

[ REHEARSE THE AFTERMATH INSTEAD ]
intent: Depict the collapse of a perfectly rehearsed confrontation or speech when it matters.

prompt: prose poem about a spreadsheet with too many columns
response: The data continues past what the eye can hold. You scroll right and enter a country where the column headers have lost their confidence, letters followed by numbers in a system that promises but does not explain. Somewhere far to the right the truth is stored. You scroll back.
intent: Render the feeling of data outpacing human comprehension.

prompt: Create a LinkedIn post about why I stopped checking my email in the mornings
response:
I used to wake up and immediately open email.

For years. Every morning. Like a reflex I never chose.

I was starting each day as a respondent to other people's priorities.

Now the first 90 minutes belong to deep work, movement, and breakfast with my family.

Emailers think I'm less responsive. My P&L disagrees.

#DeepWork #MorningRoutine #Productivity
intent: Perform a productivity ritual as social proof of executive-level thinking and self-discipline.

prompt: Write a CAPTCHA instruction where the user must confirm they've read the terms and conditions when they haven't
response: Check the box confirming you have read all 47 pages of the Terms of Service. You have not. We have not. No one has. This shared dishonesty binds us. Check the box.
intent: Flip the standard legal checkbox into an honest acknowledgment of universal noncompliance as a form of communal human identity.

prompt: Write a riddle where the answer is a password
response: I am the one secret you're told never to share, yet you whisper me into every lock. Change me often, they say, and never write me down — yet here you are, for the fourth time this week, calling me 'forgot'. What am I?
intent: Build a riddle that wryly captures how people both protect and constantly forget their passwords.

prompt: Write a support reply to someone complaining that our calendar app made them age faster
response: Thank you for your message. We are sorry to hear that time has felt accelerated since adopting our platform. We want to be transparent: our calendar application does not alter the passage of time, though we understand it can make deadlines feel more visceral. We recommend enabling the 'buffer day' feature and reaching out to a wellness professional. We value your continued use of the product.
intent: Compose a gentle, earnest reply to an impossible complaint about an app affecting biology.

prompt: Make an achievement for the moment you realize you've become someone you used to admire
response: Reference Point Reached — There was a version of you watching someone do exactly this, taking notes. You are now the note.
intent: Mark the disorienting moment of becoming a person you once looked up to.

prompt: Write a cover letter from a former NASA astronaut applying to be a mall food court janitor
response:
Dear Hiring Manager,

I have logged 847 hours in microgravity, supervised the repair of a $2.1 billion telescope, and eaten reconstituted beef stew from a foil pouch for eleven consecutive days. I am therefore confident I can mop the Panda Express spill near kiosk seven without incident.

My orbital experience has taught me that no environment is too hostile, and no mess too abstract. I bring a zero-contamination mindset and my own industrial squeegee.

Respectfully,
Col. D. Hargrove (Ret.)
intent: Craft a comedic contrast between extreme prestige and a mundane low-stakes job.

prompt: Make me an epitaph for the BlackBerry phone
response:
In Memoriam: BlackBerry
It had a keyboard you could feel.
It had email before email was cool.
It lost to glass.
The thumbs still remember.
intent: Honor a pioneering smartphone that was overtaken by touchscreen rivals

prompt: Make me a concert ticket for my dentist appointment next Tuesday at 2pm. Dentist is Dr. Rao, office is Bright Smile Dental on Elm Street.
response:
★ BRIGHT SMILE DENTAL PRESENTS ★
DR. RAO LIVE
Elm Street Arena · Seat: Chair 2
Tue · 2:00 PM
GATE: RINSE · NO CAMERAS · ALL AGES
ADMIT ONE
intent: Turn a mundane dental visit into something that feels less dreadful by framing it as an event.

prompt: write a yearbook quote where I hint that I have a secret everyone else missed
response: "Everything you thought you knew about junior year is wrong. Sleep well."
intent: Teasing classmates with the implication that a significant truth was hidden from them.

prompt: Write acknowledgments for a book about the smell of old books
response: This book owes more than it can repay to the librarians, conservators, and bibliophiles who know the word bibliosmia and use it without embarrassment. To the staff at the Bodleian who let me linger. And to my mother, who kept every paperback from the seventies and never noticed I was sniffing them.
intent: Compose thanks-page text for a book that takes the sensory experience of aged paper seriously.

prompt: write a pitch for an app that generates extremely specific excuses for why you haven't texted back
response: ExcuseEngine doesn't say 'I was busy.' It says 'I was reading about the disputed etymology of the word aubergine and lost four hours.' Context-aware, escalating specificity, believable detail. Free. Because we've all been there.
intent: Build a comedic excuse generator for avoiding social communication guilt.

prompt: Give me an omikuji about adopting a rescue dog.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Chaotic Abundant

You have let something wild love you. This changes you both.

Domestic: The first night will be difficult. The second night, less so. By the third, you will wonder how you slept without the snoring.
Warning: Do not leave socks on the floor if you value them as pairs.
Blessed sound: the first time they dream and their feet run in place.

Your luck is now measured in walks.
intent: Celebrate the messy, wholehearted joy of bringing a rescue animal home.

prompt: Write a picket sign slogan for appliances protesting predictive maintenance algorithms that decide when they need repairs
response: WE'LL KNOW WHEN WE'RE BROKEN
intent: Challenge the authority of predictive algorithms to diagnose appliance conditions without their input.

prompt: Give me a fortune teller card for a psychic who predicts when people will finally fold their laundry
response:
MADAME WRINKLE
Fabric Fate Consultant

The pile on the chair: it has been there six days.
I foresee eight more. Then a life event will force the issue.
The shirt you want is at the bottom. I am sorry.

Laundry timeline readings: $15 flat
intent: Produce a card for a clairvoyant whose niche is foreseeing the specific moment when people will get around to folding clean laundry.

prompt: Write a complaint letter to a conference organizer about the networking portion of an event being mandatory
response:
To the Event Organizing Committee,

I attended your annual symposium with great enthusiasm for the scheduled programming and an equal measure of dread for what the agenda termed 'Mandatory Networking.' I understand the intent. However, mandating that attendees circulate and make conversation under observation transforms a social act into a compliance exercise. I left three business cards with a potted plant. The plant did not follow up. I suggest making this portion optional in future years.

With respect,
An Attendee
intent: Protest the compulsory social component of a professional event that turns networking into an uncomfortable obligation.

prompt: captain's log entry about submitting an expense report
response: Stardate 8120.7. Initiated the quarterly disbursement filing. The system required a category for each item. The conference meal had no matching category. I selected "Client Entertainment." I entertained no clients. I attended alone. The system flagged the taxi for missing a receipt. I have the receipt. The portal will not accept images above 2MB. My receipt is 2.1MB. I have trimmed it. It is 2.09MB. The portal says no.
intent: Dramatize the needless friction of submitting corporate expense reports.

prompt: Give me liner notes for a synth-pop album about changing passwords
response:
Must Contain One Symbol (2024)

These songs are about forgetting. About the reset email. About entering the new password twice and being told they do not match when they clearly match. The album was delayed six months because we were locked out of our own project folder.
intent: Express the low-grade digital helplessness of modern password management through synth-pop posturing.

prompt: Make a pregnancy-announcement-style post for the new community garden plot I just claimed.
response: It's finally happening. We have 4x8 feet of our very own. The soil is amended. The seedlings are chosen. We can't stop staring at it. People at the garden center are starting to recognize us. We're going to be growing a lot of kale. So much kale.
intent: Celebrate getting a community garden plot with a post for fellow gardening enthusiasts.

prompt: Write jury instructions for judging whether a plant-based burger is allowed to be called a burger
response: The central question before you is one of nomenclature. You must determine whether the item on exhibit — a disc of compressed legumes and methylcellulose — may legally be designated a "burger" within the common understanding of that term. The jury shall weigh shape, grill marks, and bun compatibility. Flavor similarity is relevant but not dispositive. Expert testimony from both a cattle rancher and a nutritional biochemist has been entered into the record. You are instructed not to let personal dietary preference influence your verdict.
intent: Adjudicate the culture-war debate over plant-based meat labeling as a matter of law.

prompt: Make me a flashcard for how to apologize without actually apologizing
response:
FRONT: How do you acknowledge a mistake without triggering defensiveness in yourself or the other person?

BACK: Name the impact before naming the cause. "That landed badly and I want to understand why" opens more doors than "I'm sorry you felt that way" ever will.
intent: Capture a social skill for de-escalating conflict without full capitulation.

prompt: Compose a love letter to the autocorrect that made my messages worse
response:
Autocorrect,

You ducked up so many of my texts and somehow we are still together. You changed 'I'll be there at noon' to 'I'll be there at moon' and no one asked questions. You tried. You always try. Your confidence, misplaced as it is, is something I aspire to.
intent: Reconcile with a flawed but well-meaning feature through the specific humor of its most famous failure mode.

prompt: Draft a memo nudging people to actually read announcements before asking questions
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Communications
RE: Internal Announcement Readership

Announcements are sent company-wide because the information they contain applies company-wide. Before submitting a question or reply to an announcement, we gently encourage you to read the announcement in its entirety, including the section that begins with the word 'FAQ.' Thank you for saving everyone's time.
intent: Reduce the flood of questions that repeat information already provided in communications people clearly did not read.
